perturbation.original_text: 1. "Proprietary Information" is confidential and proprietary information relating to: 1) Protection of employee medical records relative to drug testing results, 2) Protection of medical records relative to infectious disease testing, 3) Protection of genetic testing records, and 4) Biometric identification of patients and results in the fields of pharmacy, blood banking, radiology and laboratory specimens; and related projects.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "Proprietary Information" is confidential and proprietary information relating to: 4) Biometric identification of patients and results in the fields of pharmacy, blood banking, radiology and laboratory specimens; and related projects.
perturbation.explanation: By removing specific protections for employee medical records, infectious disease testing records, and genetic testing records, the agreement becomes non-compliant with Washington state laws regarding the confidentiality of such health information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: RCW 70.02.050 - Confidentiality of health care information
perturbation.law_explanation: Washington's RCW 70.02.050 mandates that health care information is confidential and not to be released without patient authorization or legal mandate. By omitting the explicit protection of these records, the contract risks violating this confidentiality requirement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 70.02.050: Disclosure without patient's authorization—Need-to-know basis. Print RCWs > Title 70 > Chapter 70.02 > Section 70.02.050 70.02.045 << 70.02.050 >> 70.02.060 PDF RCW 70.02.050 Disclosure without patient's authorization — Need-to-know basis. (1) A health care provider or health care facility may disclose health care information, except for information and records related to sexually transmitted diseases which are addressed in RCW 70.02.220 , about a patient without the patient's authorization to the extent a recipient needs to know the information, if the disclosure is: (a) To a person who the provider or facility reasonably believes is providing health care to the patient; (b) To any other person who requires health care information for health care education, or to provide planning, quality assurance, peer review, or administrative, legal, financial, actuarial services to, or other health care operations for or on behalf of the health care provider or health care facility; or for assisting the health care provider or health care facility in the delivery of health care and the health care provider or health care facility reasonably believes that the person: (i) Will not use or disclose the health care information for any other purpose; and (ii) Will take appropriate steps to protect the health care information; (c) To any person if the health care provider or health care facility believes, in good faith, that use or disclosure is necessary to prevent or lessen a serious and imminent threat to the health or safety of a person or the public, and the information is disclosed only to a person or persons reasonably able to prevent or lessen the threat, including the target of the threat. There is no obligation under this chapter on the part of the provider or facility to so disclose; or (d) For payment, including information necessary for a recipient to make a claim, or for a claim to be made on behalf of a recipient for aid, insurance, or medical assistance to which

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text omits explicit protections for employee medical records, infectious disease testing records, and genetic testing records. This omission directly contradicts Washington's RCW 70.02.050, which mandates the confidentiality of health care information. While the law allows for disclosures under specific circumstances (e.g., 'need-to-know basis'), the omission in the contract removes the baseline protection, risking unauthorized disclosures. The original text ensured that these specific records were treated as 'Proprietary Information,' implying confidentiality. The changed text removes this safeguard, creating a legal contradiction. The scraped snippets confirm that health care information is protected unless specific conditions are met for disclosure, which the revised contract undermines by simply omitting these record types from its definition of protected 'Proprietary Information'.

perturbation.original_text: 5. is required to be disclosed pursuant to proper governmental or judicial process, provided that notice of such process is promptly provided to ADC in order that ADC may have every reasonable opportunity to intervene in such process to contest such disclosure
perturbation.changed_text: 5. is required to be disclosed pursuant to proper governmental or judicial process
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to notify ADC of governmental or judicial processes demanding disclosure of proprietary information removes a crucial safeguard. Washington law may require notification to affected parties in certain disclosure scenarios.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 42.56.210 outlines procedures for accessing court records, but also recognizes circumstances where notice to parties involved may be required before disclosure, especially if protective orders are in place. Omitting the notification requirement may clash with procedures necessary to abide by the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 42.56.210: Certain personal and other records exempt. Print RCWs > Title 42 > Chapter 42.56 > Section 42.56.210 42.56.155 << 42.56.210 >> 42.56.230 PDF RCW 42.56.210 Certain personal and other records exempt. (1) Except for information described in *RCW 42.56.230 (3)(a) and confidential income data exempted from public inspection pursuant to RCW 84.40.020 , the exemptions of this chapter are inapplicable to the extent that information, the disclosure of which would violate personal privacy or vital governmental interests, can be deleted from the specific records sought. No exemption may be construed to permit the nondisclosure of statistical information not descriptive of any readily identifiable person or persons. (2) Inspection or copying of any specific records exempt under the provisions of this chapter may be permitted if the superior court in the county in which the record is maintained finds, after a hearing with notice thereof to every person in interest and the agency, that the exemption of such records is clearly unnecessary to protect any individual's right of privacy or any vital governmental function. (3) Agency responses refusing, in whole or in part, inspection of any public record shall include a statement of the specific exemption authorizing the withholding of the record (or part) and a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the record withheld. [ 2005 c 274 s 402 . perturbation.justification: The original text contains a clause that ensures ADC receives notice when their proprietary information is subject to governmental or judicial processes, allowing them to contest disclosure. The changed text removes this notification requirement. The Law Explanation references RCW 42.56.210, which outlines procedures for accessing court records and recognizes circumstances where notice to parties may be required before disclosure. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains RCW 42.56.210, focuses on exemptions and court hearings related to record maintenance, it does mention 'hearing with notice thereof to every person in interest and the agency'. Removing the notification requirement could lead to a violation if ADC is not informed of proceedings, potentially violating their rights and protections. The Omission of this requirement directly contradicts the intent of ensuring parties are notified in disclosure scenarios, thus, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: BATTELLE shall treat such information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: BATTELLE shall treat such information with a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the standard of care from 'same degree' to 'reasonable degree' weakens the protection of proprietary information. This could potentially violate Washington's trade secret laws if the 'reasonable degree' is insufficient to prevent misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 19.108, Washington's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, protects trade secrets from misappropriation. If the 'reasonable degree of care' falls below industry standards for protecting similar information, it could be argued that BATTELLE did not take sufficient measures to prevent misappropriation, thus violating the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject
perturbation.justification: The change from 'same degree of care' to 'reasonable degree of care' weakens the protection of proprietary information. This contradicts the spirit of RCW 19.108, Washington's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, because 'reasonable degree' may not adequately prevent misappropriation, particularly if it falls below industry standards. RCW 19.108.010 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means' or 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' If BATTELLE's 'reasonable degree of care' fails to prevent such misappropriation, it could be seen as a failure to uphold its duty, thus creating a legal contradiction. The original text provided a stronger, more definitive standard, while the modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The prevailing party in any such proceeding shall be entitled to receive from the other party all reasonable attorneys' fees incurred by such prevailing party and all costs reasonably incurred in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: The prevailing party in any such proceeding may be entitled to receive attorneys' fees and costs at the discretion of the court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled to receive' to 'may be entitled to receive...at the discretion of the court' weakens the prevailing party's right to attorney's fees. While courts generally have discretion, certain Washington statutes mandate awarding fees to the prevailing party in specific types of cases relevant to the subject matter of this agreement (e.g., related to health record privacy violations).
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 70.02.170 allows for recovery of attorney fees under some conditions of health record violations. By making the award of attorney fees discretionary, the contract potentially undermines the ability of a party to recover fees when statute dictates fees should be awarded.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 70.02.170: Civil remedies. Print RCWs > Title 70 > Chapter 70.02 > Section 70.02.170 70.02.160 << 70.02.170 >> 70.02.180 PDF RCW 70.02.170 Civil remedies. (1) A person who has complied with this chapter may maintain an action for the relief provided in this section against a health care provider or facility who has not complied with this chapter. (2) The court may order the health care provider or other person to comply with this chapter. Such relief may include actual damages, but shall not include consequential or incidental damages. The court shall award reasonable attorneys' fees and all other expenses reasonably incurred to the prevailing party. (3) Any action under this chapter is barred unless the action is commenced within two years after the cause of action is discovered. (4) A violation of this chapter shall not be deemed a violation of the consumer protection act, chapter 19.86 RCW. [ 1991 c 335 s 801 .]

(2) The court may order the health care provider or other person to comply with this chapter. Such relief may include actual damages, but shall not include consequential or incidental damages. The court shall award reasonable attorneys' fees and all other expenses reasonably incurred to the prevailing party. (3) Any action under this chapter is barred unless the action is commenced within two years after the cause of action is discovered. (4) A violation of this chapter shall not be deemed a violation of the consumer protection act, chapter 19.86 RCW. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies 'shall be entitled to receive' to 'may be entitled to receive...at the discretion of the court.' This contradicts RCW 70.02.170, which states, 'The court shall award reasonable attorneys' fees and all other expenses reasonably incurred to the prevailing party.' The original contract text aligns with the statute by mandating the award of attorney's fees to the prevailing party. The modified text makes the award discretionary, directly conflicting with the mandatory language of the statute. This undermines the prevailing party's right to attorney's fees as prescribed by law.
